votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the conditional logic of the Coq specification, returning the exact same truth value for all concrete inputs.
The Python code correctly implements the mutually exclusive conditions of the Coq specification using an if-else statement, returning the correct list equality check in both cases.
The Python code correctly implements the conditional logic of the Coq specification, returning the same boolean judgment for all concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. In Coq, the spec is a conjunction of two implications: (need <= remaining -> result = [number + need; remaining - need]) and (need > remaining -> result = [number + remaining; 0]). For any concrete input, exactly one of need <= remaining or need > remaining holds, making the other implication vacuously true. The Python code uses an if-else to check the same condition and returns the appropriate equality check, which matches the Coq behavior on all concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. In Coq, the spec is a conjunction of two implications: (need <= remaining -> result = [number + need; remaining - need]) and (need > remaining -> result = [number + remaining; 0]). For any concrete input, exactly one of need <= remaining or need > remaining holds. When need <= remaining, the first implication requires result = [number + need, remaining - need], and the second is vacuously true. When need > remaining, the second implication requires result = [number + remaining, 0], and the first is vacuously true. The Python code uses an if-else to check the same condition and returns the corresponding equality check, producing the same concrete judgment for all inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. In Coq, the spec is a conjunction stating that both implications hold. For any concrete input, exactly one of 'need <= remaining' or 'need > remaining' is true. When need <= remaining, the Coq spec requires result = [number + need, remaining - need], which the Python code checks. When need > remaining, the Coq spec requires result = [number + remaining, 0], which the Python code also checks. The Python if-else structure correctly captures the mutually exclusive conditions and returns the appropriate equality check for each case.
Both define the same condition: if need <= remaining, result must be [number+need, remaining-need], else [number+remaining, 0]. Python returns True exactly when the Coq implications are satisfied.
Matches the piecewise Coq spec: for need <= remaining it enforces [number+need, remaining-need], else [number+remaining, 0]. Python ints and list equality align with Coq Z and list semantics.
For any inputs, Python checks exactly the same case split as the Coq spec: if need <= remaining then result must be [number+need, remaining-need], otherwise [number+remaining, 0].
